title: Pre-Islamic Arabia
bullets: Pre-Islamic Arabia was about a million square miles of mostly desert with two to three million people, organized in Bedouin tribes that owed loyalty only to clan, conducted blood-vengeance feuds across generations, and recognized no king or written law above tribal authority.

The Quraysh tribe ran Mecca through control of the Kaaba — a polytheistic shrine housing roughly 360 idols representing the gods of various Arabian tribes — and through the two great annual caravans that linked Yemen and Syria during the four sacred months when tribal warfare paused for pilgrim traffic.

Two clans of the Quraysh would shape Islamic history: the Banu Hashim (Muhammad's clan, materially modest, religiously serious) and the Banu Umayya (richer, politically dominant, the eventual founders of the Umayyad dynasty after Muhammad's death).

Yathrib — the oasis town 210 miles north of Mecca that would be renamed Medina after the Hijra — was an unusually mixed settlement with two Arab tribes (Aws and Khazraj) locked in a long feud and three substantial Jewish tribes who had Arabized while keeping their religion.

The Lakhmids of al-Hira served Sasanian Persia as Christian Arab buffer kings; the Ghassanids of southern Syria served Byzantine Rome the same way; both buffer kingdoms collapsed in the early seventh century, leaving the imperial frontiers undefended just as the Arabs were about to attack.

Pre-Islamic Arabian religion was a mix of tribal polytheism (Allah, al-Lat, al-Uzza, Manat), Christianity in the south and the buffer kingdoms, Judaism in the Hijazi oases, and a small monotheistic tendency called the Hanifs that Muhammad's grandfather and other Meccan elders were already drifting toward.

Both the Sasanian Persian and Byzantine Roman empires were exhausted by 600 CE after the most destructive Roman-Persian war yet (602–628 CE) — broke, depopulated, and militarily depleted, with no reserves left for what was about to come from Arabia.

The peninsula's role had always been to supply spices, gold, slaves, and Bedouin mercenaries to the great states around it; nothing about the world Muhammad was born into in 570 looks like a launching pad for a religion that would conquer half of it within a century.
subtitle: tribal Bedouin society, the Quraysh at Mecca, the Kaaba, and two exhausted empires

title: Muhammad
bullets: Almost everything we know about Muhammad's life comes from biographies written 100 to 250 years after his death; the major outlines (birth in Mecca, marriage to Khadija, Hijra to Medina, conquest of Mecca) are reliably attested, while specific dialogue and date precision are much shakier.

Muhammad was orphaned young, raised by his uncle Abu Talib in modest circumstances, and worked in the caravan trade until the wealthy widow Khadija proposed marriage to him around 595 — fifteen years his senior, she was his financial and emotional anchor and the first person to accept his revelations.

Around 610 in a cave on Mount Hira above Mecca, Muhammad had the visionary experience that Islamic tradition treats as the founding moment of the religion: the angel Gabriel appearing and commanding him to recite the verses that became Chapter 96 of the Quran.

In 622 — fleeing Quraysh persecution after his protector uncle Abu Talib died — Muhammad and his followers emigrated north to Yathrib, which was renamed Medina; the Hijra became year 1 of the Islamic calendar, dating not from Muhammad's birth but from the founding of the first Muslim political community.

At Medina, Muhammad drafted the Constitution of Medina — a treaty among Muslims, Jews, and Arab tribes that established him as arbitrator and recognized Jewish religious autonomy, one of the earliest written constitutions in any society.

Three battles defined the Medinan period: Badr in 624 (the founding victory, taken as divine confirmation), Uhud in 625 (a near-defeat that became the founding cautionary tale), and the Battle of the Trench in 627 (Persian-style trench fortifications stopped a 10,000-strong Quraysh siege of Medina).

In 630 Muhammad marched on Mecca with an army of 10,000, took the city essentially without fighting, smashed the 360 idols in the Kaaba, declared a general amnesty for nearly all his former opponents, and rededicated the Kaaba to monotheistic worship — fixing Mecca's permanent status as Islam's holiest city.

Muhammad died at Medina in June 632 in the apartment of his youngest wife Aisha, with no surviving son and — according to Sunnis — no clear successor designated; the argument over succession produced the entire subsequent history of Sunni-Shia conflict, including the Karbala killing fifty years later.
subtitle: the prophet's life, the Hijra, the Quranic revelations, and the founding of the Muslim community

title: The Rashidun Caliphs
bullets: Sunni tradition treats all four Rashidun ('rightly-guided') caliphs as legitimate; Shia tradition rejects the first three (Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman) as usurpers and recognizes only Ali, with the split going back to whether Muhammad designated Ali as his successor at Ghadir Khumm shortly before his death.

Abu Bakr (632–634) spent his two-year reign crushing the Ridda Wars — the revolts of Arab tribes who tried to leave the Muslim federation after Muhammad's death — through campaigns by the gifted general Khalid ibn al-Walid, locking Arabia into the new state.

Under Umar ibn al-Khattab (634–644) the Arab armies destroyed the Sasanian Empire at the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah in 636, took Syria from the Byzantines at the Battle of Yarmouk three months earlier, captured Jerusalem in 637, and conquered Egypt by 642 — the most spectacular phase of conquest in Islamic history.

The conquests succeeded because both empires had just spent twenty-six years exhausting each other, the local populations were alienated by Constantinopolitan and Sasanian religious persecution, the Arab armies were tactically extraordinary, and the conquest was rapid while the conversion to Islam took centuries.

Umar created the diwan registry of Arab Muslim warriors entitled to conquest stipends and founded the garrison cities of Kufa and Basra in Iraq and Fustat in Egypt — keeping Arab settlers concentrated and separated from the conquered populations.

Around 650 Caliph Uthman commissioned the canonical text of the Quran — the Uthmanic codex — and ordered all variant copies destroyed; modern textual scholarship confirms that the Quran has been remarkably well preserved since this codification, more so than most major religious texts.

Uthman's twelve-year reign ended in his murder in his own house in Medina in 656 by rebels from Egypt, triggering the First Fitna — Muslims fighting Muslims for the first time, including the Battle of the Camel against Aisha's faction and the Battle of Siffin against Mu'awiya's Syrian army.

Ali ibn Abi Talib was assassinated in 661 by a Kharijite at the great mosque in Kufa; his elder son Hasan abdicated to Mu'awiya in exchange for a pension and the broken promise that the caliphate would revert to Ali's descendants — the deal that made the Umayyad dynasty hereditary.
subtitle: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali — the lightning conquests and the first Muslim civil war

title: The Sunni-Shia Split
bullets: Mu'awiya's nineteen-year reign (661–680) ran the caliphate from Damascus through inherited Byzantine bureaucracy and naval power, and ended with the most consequential decision of the dynasty: nominating his son Yazid as successor, converting the caliphate from an elected office into a hereditary monarchy.

Husayn ibn Ali — Muhammad's grandson, son of Ali and Fatima — refused to swear allegiance to Yazid in 680 and rode toward Kufa in Iraq with a small family entourage, expecting Iraqi Shia partisans to rise in his support; instead the Umayyad governor intimidated the network into silence.

On 10 October 680 — the day of Ashura — an Umayyad force surrounded Husayn at Karbala, cut him off from water, killed his male relatives one by one over a week, killed Husayn last after he fought alone among the bodies of his family, and decapitated his corpse to parade through the streets of Damascus.

Husayn's killing transformed an essentially political dispute about succession into a permanent religious division about authority and the meaning of suffering, and the annual Ashura mourning became the central ritual of Shia religious life — observed for thirteen centuries with weeping, chest-beating, and funeral processions.

'Every day is Ashura, every land is Karbala' is one of the most-quoted modern Shia political slogans, invoked in the 1979 Iranian Revolution against the Shah, in the Iran-Iraq War, and by Hezbollah and Iraqi Shia movements in the 2000s — the Karbala memory has remained politically powerful for thirteen centuries.

Sunni and Shia diverge on five substantive issues: religious authority (consensus of scholars vs. divinely designated Imams), the imamate lineage, legal sources (Hanafi/Maliki/Shafi'i/Hanbali vs. Ja'fari schools), the identity of the Mahdi, and the role of mourning rituals and intercession.

Twelver Shias (the largest branch, the official religion of Iran since the Safavid conversion of 1501) recognize twelve Imams ending with the Mahdi who went into occultation in 874; Ismaili Shias split over Ja'far al-Sadiq's succession in 765; Zaydi Shias recognize a different fifth Imam and are concentrated today in Yemen.

The Iran-Saudi rivalry, the Hezbollah-Israel conflict, the Iraq Sunni-Shia tensions, the Yemeni Houthi war — all of this operates in part on theological-political fault lines established between 632 and 750, with the killing of Husayn at Karbala as the central foundational event.
subtitle: Karbala, the killing of Husayn, the formation of Shia Islam, and a thirteen-century political fault line

title: Damascus
bullets: Mu'awiya kept his capital at Damascus rather than returning to Medina, importing the Byzantine administrative apparatus — Greek-speaking civil servants, existing tax registers, Roman urban infrastructure — into the new caliphate's governance and reshaping Islamic political culture in a Roman-imperial direction.

The Second Fitna (680–692) saw Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr run a counter-caliphate from Mecca for twelve years; Yazid I's army bombarded the Kaaba itself with stone projectiles and burned it during the siege in 683 — a theological catastrophe the Umayyads would never live down.

Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan (685–705) was the second great organizer of the Umayyad state; he won the Second Fitna by sending the ruthless governor al-Hajjaj to crucify ibn al-Zubayr's corpse on the gates of Mecca in 692, then started the structural reforms that turned the caliphate into a recognizably Islamic empire.

Around 695 Abd al-Malik decreed that the bureaucracy must shift from inherited Greek and Pahlavi to Arabic — a reform that took a generation to complete but locked Arabic in as the administrative language of the Middle East and produced the script reforms (dots, vowel marks) that are still used today.

In 696 Abd al-Malik issued the first purely Islamic gold dinar, replacing Byzantine and Sasanian coinage with aniconic Arabic-inscribed coins that would remain the gold currency of medieval Eurasian commerce for the next four centuries.

The Dome of the Rock, completed by Abd al-Malik in 691 on the abandoned Temple Mount in Jerusalem, was the first major piece of monumental Islamic architecture and a deliberate theological statement against Christian Jerusalem, with mosaics including extended Quranic passages denying the divinity of Christ.

The Umayyads were widely accused — by their critics and by later Abbasid propaganda — of being secular Arab kings rather than Muslim caliphs, with their silk robes, Roman-style desert palaces, hunting falcons, and Byzantine court ceremony all marshaled as evidence by the political opposition.

The structural problem the dynasty never solved was the mawali grievance: non-Arab Muslim converts paid the same taxes as non-Muslims and were excluded from the Arab-only diwan stipend system, an injustice that the religion's egalitarian rhetoric continued to expose with growing force.
subtitle: Mu'awiya, Abd al-Malik, the Arabization of administration, and the gold dinar

title: The Conquests West and East
bullets: In a single decade (705–715) the Umayyads conquered Spain, Sind, and Transoxiana, more than doubling the empire's geographic reach in three simultaneous campaigns at opposite ends of the known world.

Tariq ibn Ziyad's small Berber-Arab force crossed the strait between Morocco and Spain in 711, defeated the Visigothic king Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete, and within seven years had taken nearly the entire Iberian Peninsula — initiating 781 years of Muslim presence on Iberian soil.

The crossing point at Gibraltar — Jabal Tariq, 'Tariq's Mountain' — still bears Tariq's name, and the conquest of Spain produced the medieval civilization of al-Andalus that gave the world the Great Mosque of Córdoba, the Alhambra, and the work of Ibn Rushd and Maimonides.

Muhammad ibn al-Qasim, a seventeen-year-old Umayyad general, conquered Sind (the lower Indus Valley, modern southeastern Pakistan) in the same year as Spain — the first foothold of Islam on the Indian subcontinent, eight centuries before the Mughal Empire would consolidate Muslim rule in northern India.

Qutayba ibn Muslim took Bukhara in 709 and Samarkand in 712, extending Umayyad control across Transoxiana and bringing Sogdian Iranian-language Silk Road cities into the Islamic world — a region that would become one of the great heartlands of Islamic civilization, producing Ferdowsi and Ibn Sina.

The Second Arab Siege of Constantinople in 717–718 sent 80,000 Umayyad troops and 1,800 ships against the Byzantine capital under Caliph Sulayman's brother Maslama; Greek fire destroyed the fleet, the Theodosian Walls held, an unusually severe winter killed thousands, and only five ships of the original 1,800 returned.

If Constantinople had fallen in 718, Arab armies would have had a clear path into Europe via the Balkans — the failure of the siege effectively halted Arab expansion into Europe by sea, and the Byzantine Empire would survive another seven hundred years as the Christian buffer for eastern Europe.

Western European textbooks have traditionally credited the Battle of Tours in 732 with stopping the Arab advance into Europe, but the strategic significance has been overstated: the raid Charles Martel defeated was probably a probe, not a serious invasion, and the Constantinople siege fourteen years earlier mattered far more.
subtitle: Spain in 711, Sind in 711, Transoxiana in 712, and the failure at Constantinople in 718

title: Architecture and the Religion
bullets: The Dome of the Rock, completed in 691, sat over a rock that Islamic tradition associated with Muhammad's Night Journey ascension, on the abandoned Temple Mount of the Jewish Second Temple (destroyed by Rome in 70 CE) — a deliberately layered claim to Jewish, Christian, and Muslim religious geography.

The Dome of the Rock used a familiar Christian architectural vocabulary — central plan, octagonal arcade, gilded dome, Roman-Byzantine mosaics — to make a building that would visually compete with Christian holy sites for religious authority, with the form Roman and the message Islamic.

The Great Mosque of Damascus (completed 715 by al-Walid I) was built on a site that had been an Aramean Hadad temple, a Roman Jupiter temple, then a Christian basilica of John the Baptist; al-Walid bought out the Christian congregation, demolished the basilica, and built the oldest surviving congregational mosque retaining its original plan.

The al-Aqsa Mosque on the southern end of the Temple Mount, built around 705 alongside the Dome of the Rock, made Jerusalem the third-holiest city in Islam after Mecca and Medina; the Quranic phrase 'al-Aqsa' became fixed to the Jerusalem building within decades of Muhammad's death.

Umayyad desert palaces — Qusayr Amra in Jordan, Khirbet al-Mafjar near Jericho, Mshatta, Anjar in Lebanon — show what the dynasty actually liked: Roman-Byzantine plans, figural and even erotic imagery, courtly luxury that was much closer to Sasanian-Byzantine royal practice than to Medinan Muslim austerity.

Three theological currents emerged in the Umayyad century: the Qadariyya argued for human free will against the predestinarian establishment, the Murji'a deferred judgment on whether sinners were still Muslims (a politically convenient position for the dynasty), and the Mu'tazila pioneered Islamic rationalism.

Hadith collection began under the late Umayyads with the methodological discipline of evaluating each report's chain of transmission (the isnad) — a critical apparatus that medieval Muslim scholars developed centuries before modern Western critical scholarship discovered comparable methods.

The dhimmi system — protection of life, property, and worship for Christians, Jews, and Zoroastrians in exchange for the jizya head tax and acceptance of Muslim political supremacy — became the Islamic framework for governing religious minorities, and ran from the seventh century through the late nineteenth-century Ottoman reforms.
subtitle: the Dome of the Rock, the Great Mosque of Damascus, early hadith, and the dhimmi system

title: The Abbasid Revolution
bullets: By the 740s the Umayyad state was failing in five separate ways simultaneously: fiscal collapse from the converting tax base, mawali grievance from non-Arab Muslims excluded from privilege, pre-Islamic Qaysi-Kalbi tribal feuding, Berber and Kharijite revolts in North Africa, and caliphal succession chaos after Hisham's death in 743.

Abu Muslim, an obscure freedman of probably Persian origin whose actual identity remains uncertain, raised the black banner of the Abbasid family at Merv in 747, organizing a coalition of mawali, Persian-Iraqi disgruntled converts, Yamani tribal clans, and Shia partisans into a unified revolutionary force.

The black banner became a long-lived symbol of Sunni messianic eschatology — 'a black banner from Khorasan' would be invoked by various jihadist movements thirteen centuries later, though the original was a dynastic logo for the descendants of Muhammad's uncle al-Abbas, not a religious symbol.

On 25 January 750 at the Battle of the Great Zab in northern Iraq, the Abbasid army from Khorasan defeated and destroyed the main Umayyad field force under Marwan II in person; Marwan fled west across Syria into Egypt and was killed in a small Egyptian village called Busir six months later.

The first Abbasid caliph Abu al-Abbas as-Saffah ('the Bloodletter,' the epithet self-chosen) invited the surviving Umayyad princes to a banquet at Damascus to negotiate their surrender, then had perhaps eighty of them murdered at table — the Abbasids hunted down the rest of the family across the empire over the following years.

Abd al-Rahman I, a twenty-year-old Umayyad survivor of the massacre, escaped by reportedly swimming the Euphrates while Abbasid horsemen shot at him from the bank; after five years on the run across North Africa he reached Spain and in 756 founded the independent Emirate of Córdoba that would last another 270 years.

The new Abbasid state moved its capital from Damascus to Baghdad (founded 762 by al-Mansur), Persianized the administration through families like the Barmakids, fixed the mawali grievance by extending equality to non-Arab Muslims, and patronized the translation of Greek philosophy into Arabic — the launching pad for the Islamic Golden Age.

The Umayyad century built the framework — the conquests, the Damascus capital, the Dome of the Rock, the Quranic codification, Arabic administration, the gold dinar, the dhimmi system — and the Abbasids inherited and built on it; the dynasty died in 750, but the Islamic civilization it founded continued and is continuing.
subtitle: the fiscal crisis, the mawali grievance, the Battle of the Zab, and the Umayyad survivor in Spain
